claim: Never create a database with SQL_ASCII encoding — it means 'no conversion', so you end up storing an unrecoverable mixture of encodings; use UTF8, and bytea for genuinely unlabeled byte soup.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|Rules are query rewrites use triggers
claim: Never use CREATE RULE — rules look like conditional logic but actually rewrite the query or add queries to it, and essentially all non-trivial rules are subtly incorrect; use a trigger.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|No table inheritance use keys or partitions
claim: Don't use table inheritance — the object-relational coupling fad it served didn't pan out; use foreign keys for relationships and native declarative partitioning for the partitioning use case.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|NOT IN subqueries are a trap
claim: Never write NOT IN (SELECT …): one NULL in the subquery silently returns zero rows (NOT(NULL) is NULL), and the planner can't anti-join it — the fallback plan is O(N²), fast in tests and catastrophic at size; rewrite as NOT EXISTS. Constant lists are safe only when a NULL can't appear.

Use names_like_this for tables and columns — Postgres folds unquoted names to lowercase, so any uppercase name commits you to quoting it always or never, and tools disagree; alias to pretty names at SELECT time instead.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|Half-open ranges not BETWEEN on timestamps
claim: Don't use BETWEEN on timestamps — it's a closed interval, so the end boundary's exact midnight lands in BOTH adjacent ranges and gets double-counted; write 'col >= start AND col < end'.

Store moments in time as timestamptz, not timestamp — timestamptz records an absolute instant (microseconds since 2000 UTC) so arithmetic across zones and DST transitions is correct; a bare timestamp is a picture of a clock, not a time.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|UTC in naive timestamps is a convention not a fact
claim: Don't store UTC values in timestamp WITHOUT time zone — the database has no way to know the column means UTC, which turns simple zone math into triple AT TIME ZONE contortions; that habit is inherited from databases with broken timezone support.

Never use the timetz type or CURRENT_TIME (which returns one) — a time-of-day with a zone but no date is semantically broken, kept only for SQL-standard compliance; use now()/CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, LOCALTIMESTAMP, CURRENT_DATE, or LOCALTIME as appropriate.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|Precision specs round into the future
claim: Don't declare timestamp(0)/timestamptz(0) — precision specs ROUND rather than truncate, so storing now() can yield a value up to half a second in the future; use date_trunc('second', …).

key: General|SQL|Postgres|Offset strings invert sign as zone names
claim: Don't pass '+04:00'-style text as a time zone NAME — Postgres reads it as a POSIX spec where positive shifts WEST (inverse of ISO); use a real zone name or AT TIME ZONE INTERVAL '04:00' (ISO literals like '…17:16+04' are fine).

Never use char(n) — values are space-padded (with surprising comparison semantics), it isn't even fixed-width in storage, and the padding makes operations SLOWER, not faster; it can also silently defeat index use when compared against text-typed parameters.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|Fixed-length codes get text plus CHECK
claim: Even for always-N-character codes (country codes, hashes), use text with CHECK(length(VALUE)=N) or a regex check — char(n) doesn't reject short values, it pads them, while the constraint actually validates format.

key: General|SQL|Postgres|text by default not varchar n
claim: Default to text — varchar(n) has identical storage and performance, so an arbitrary limit like varchar(20) buys nothing except a future production error on a long surname; when a field genuinely needs constraints, a CHECK expresses min length, max length, and format together.

Don't use the money type — it stores no currency (meaning changes if lc_monetary changes), can't represent fractions of a cent, and rounds its own way; use numeric with the currency in an adjacent column.
Use GENERATED … AS IDENTITY instead of serial for new schemas (Postgres 10+) — serial's implicit sequences make schema, dependency, and permission management needlessly cumbersome.

Never use trust authentication on host/hostssl lines in production — anyone who can reach the port can claim to be any user including the superuser; use scram-sha-256 (CI containers and localhost-only dev are the narrow exceptions).

key: General|SQL|Postgres|psql password flag lies to you
claim: Don't invoke psql with -W/--password — it forces a password prompt even when the server doesn't want one, masking the real auth method (peer) and letting wrong passwords appear to 'work'; psql prompts on its own when a password is actually required.
